1.translation: Although I could have included the adventures of the Chevalier des Grieux in my Memoirs, it seemed to me that, as there was no necessary connection, the reader would find greater satisfaction in seeing them separately. A narrative of this length would have interrupted the thread of my own story for too long. However far I am from claiming the quality of an exact writer, I am not unaware that a narration must be freed from circumstances that would render it heavy and cumbersome. This is Horace's precept: 'Ut jam nunc dicat jam nunc debentia dici Pleraque differat, ac proesens in tempus omittat.' There is not even a need for such grave authority to prove so simple a truth; for common sense is the primary source of this rule. If the public has found anything agreeable and interesting in the story of my life, I dare promise them that they will be no less satisfied with this addition. They will see, in the conduct of M. des Grieux, a terrible example of the force of passions. I have to paint a young blind man, who refuses to be happy, only to plunge voluntarily into the deepest misfortunes; who, with all the qualities that form the most brilliant merit, prefers, by choice, an obscure and wandering life, to all the advantages of fortune and nature; who foresees his misfortunes, without wishing to avoid them; who feels them and is overwhelmed by them, without profiting from the remedies constantly offered to him and which could at any moment end them; finally, an ambiguous character, a mixture of virtues and vices, a perpetual contrast of good sentiments and bad actions.

2.translation: Such is the background of the picture I present. People of good sense will not view a work of this nature as useless labor. Besides the pleasure of agreeable reading, one will find few events therein that cannot serve for the instruction of morals; and it is, in my opinion, rendering a considerable service to the public to instruct them while entertaining them. One cannot reflect on the precepts of morality without being astonished to see them simultaneously esteemed and neglected; and one wonders about the reason for this oddity of the human heart, which makes it appreciate ideas of good and perfection, yet from which it distances itself in practice. If people of a certain intellectual and refined disposition wish to examine what is the most common subject of their conversations, or even of their solitary musings, it will be easy for them to notice that they almost always turn on some moral considerations. The sweetest moments of their lives are those they spend, either alone or with a friend, conversing openly about the charms of virtue, the delights of friendship, the means of achieving happiness, the weaknesses of human nature that distance us from it, and the remedies that can cure them. Horace and Boileau mark this kind of conversation as one of the finest features with which they compose the image of a happy life.

3.translation: How does it happen, then, that one falls so easily from these lofty speculations and finds oneself so soon back at the level of common men? I am mistaken if the reason I am about to give does not fully explain this contradiction between our ideas and our conduct; it is that, all moral precepts being merely vague and general principles, it is very difficult to apply them specifically to the details of manners and actions. Let us illustrate with an example. Well-born souls feel that gentleness and humanity are amiable virtues, and are inclined by nature to practice them; but when it comes to putting them into practice, they often remain hesitant. Is it truly the right occasion? Does one really know what its measure should be? Is one not mistaken about the object? A hundred difficulties arise. One fears becoming a dupe by wanting to be benevolent and liberal; of passing for weak by appearing too tender and too sensitive; in a word, of exceeding or not adequately fulfilling duties that are too obscurely contained within the general notions of humanity and gentleness. In this uncertainty, only experience or example can reasonably determine the heart's inclination. Now, experience is not an advantage that everyone is free to acquire; it depends on the different situations in which one is placed by fortune.

4.dayTitle: Justifying the Narrative, Meeting Des Grieux
4.translation: Therefore, only example remains that can serve as a rule for many people in the exercise of virtue. It is precisely for this kind of reader that works such as this one can be of extreme utility, at least when they are written by a person of honor and good sense. Every fact reported therein is a degree of light, an instruction that supplements experience; every adventure is a model according to which one can shape oneself; it only needs to be adjusted to the circumstances in which one finds oneself. The entire work is a moral treatise, agreeably reduced to practice. A severe reader will perhaps be offended to see me take up the pen again, at my age, to write about adventures of fortune and love; but, if the reflection I have just made is sound, it justifies me; if it is false, my error will be my excuse. FIRST PART I am obliged to take my reader back to the time in my life when I first met the Chevalier des Grieux. This was about six months before my departure for Spain. Although I rarely left my solitude, the indulgence I had for my daughter sometimes led me to various short journeys, which I shortened as much as possible. One day I was returning from Rouen, where she had asked me to go to solicit a matter at the Parliament of Normandy for the succession of some lands to which I had left her claims on my maternal grandfather's side.

vocabulary.translation: to supplement, to make up for
to take up the pen again

5.translation: Having resumed my journey via Evreux, where I spent the first night, I arrived the next day for dinner at Pacy, which is five or six leagues distant. I was surprised, upon entering this town, to see all its inhabitants in alarm. They were rushing from their houses to run in a crowd to the door of a shabby inn, in front of which stood two covered carriages. The horses, still harnessed and appearing to steam with fatigue and heat, indicated that these two vehicles had only just arrived. I stopped for a moment to inquire about the source of the tumult; but I gained little clarification from a curious populace, who paid no attention to my questions and kept pressing towards the inn, pushing one another with much confusion. Finally, an archer, wearing a bandolier and with his musket on his shoulder, having appeared at the door, I waved him over to me. I asked him to tell me the reason for this disturbance. "It's nothing, sir," he told me; "it's a dozen prostitutes whom I am escorting, with my companions, to Le Havre-de-Grâce, where we will have them embarked for America. There are a few pretty ones among them, and that, apparently, is what excites the curiosity of these good peasants." I would have passed on after this explanation, had I not been stopped by the exclamations of an old woman who was leaving the inn wringing her hands and crying that it was a barbarous thing, a thing that inspired horror and pity.

6.translation: What is it about, then? I asked her. "Ah! sir, come in," she replied, "and see if this sight isn't capable of breaking one's heart!" Curiosity made me dismount from my horse, which I left with my groom. I entered with difficulty, pushing through the crowd, and I did indeed see something quite moving. Among the twelve girls who were chained six by six by the waist, there was one whose demeanor and appearance were so little in conformity with her condition that, in any other state, I would have taken her for a person of the highest rank. Her sadness and the dirtiness of her linen and clothes disfigured her so little that her sight inspired in me both respect and pity. She nevertheless tried to turn, as much as her chain allowed, to hide her face from the eyes of the spectators. The effort she made to hide herself was so natural that it seemed to stem from a feeling of modesty. As the six guards who accompanied this unfortunate group were also in the room, I took the chief aside and asked him for some information about the fate of this beautiful girl. He could only give me very general ones. "We took her from the Hospital," he told me, "by order of the Lieutenant General of Police. There's no indication that she would have been confined there for her good deeds."

7.dayTitle: Manon's Companion and Des Grieux's Grief
7.translation: I questioned her several times on the road, but she insists on not answering me. Yet, although I haven't received orders to treat her more leniently than the others, I still feel some consideration for her, because it seems to me that she is a little better than her companions. "Here is a young man," added the archer, "who could inform you better than I about the cause of her disgrace; he has followed her from Paris, almost without ceasing to weep for a moment. It must be her brother or her lover." I turned towards the corner of the room where this young man was seated. He appeared buried in a profound reverie. I have never seen a more vivid image of sorrow. He was dressed very simply; but one could distinguish, at first glance, a man of noble birth and education. I approached him. He stood up; and I discovered in his eyes, in his face, and in all his movements, an air so refined and noble that I felt naturally inclined to wish him well. "Let me not trouble you," I said to him, sitting down next to him. "Would you kindly satisfy my curiosity to know this beautiful person, who does not seem to me made for the sad state in which I see her?"

8.dayTitle: Des Grieux's Unfortunate Pursuit of Manon
8.translation: He replied honestly that he could not tell me who she was without revealing his own identity, and that he had strong reasons to wish to remain unknown. "Nevertheless, I can tell you what these wretches are certainly not ignorant of," he continued, pointing to the archers, "it is that I love her with such a violent passion that it makes me the most unfortunate of all men. I employed every means in Paris to obtain her freedom. Pleas, cunning, and force were useless to me; I decided to follow her, even if she were to go to the ends of the earth. I will embark with her; I will go to America. But what is of the utmost inhumanity, these cowardly rascals," he added, speaking of the archers, "will not allow me to approach her. My intention was to attack them openly, a few leagues from Paris. I had associated myself with four men who had promised me their help for a considerable sum. The traitors left me alone in their hands and departed with my money. The impossibility of succeeding by force made me lay down my arms. I proposed to the archers that they at least allow me to follow them, offering to reward them. The desire for gain made them consent. They wanted to be paid each time they granted me the liberty to speak to my mistress. My purse was exhausted in a short time, and now that I am without a penny, they have the barbarity to brutally repel me when I take a step towards her."

9.translation: Moments ago, despite their threats, they had the audacity to point their muskets at me. To satisfy their greed and enable me to continue my journey on foot, I am compelled to sell a poor horse that has served as my mount until now. Although he recounted this tale with apparent composure, a few tears fell as he finished. This adventure struck me as most extraordinary and touching. 'I do not press you,' I said to him, 'to reveal the secret of your affairs, but if I can be of any use to you, I gladly offer my services.' 'Alas!' he replied, 'I see no glimmer of hope. I must submit to the full rigor of my fate. I will go to America. There, at least, I shall be free with the one I love. I have written to a friend who will arrange for some aid to be sent to me at Le Havre-de-Grâce. My only concern is how to get there and to provide for this poor creature,' he added, looking sadly at his mistress, 'some relief on the journey.' 'Well,' I said, 'I will resolve your predicament. Here is some money, which I beg you to accept. I am sorry I cannot serve you in any other way.' I gave him four gold louis, without the guards noticing, for I surmised that if they knew he had this sum, they would charge him more dearly for their help.

10.translation: It even occurred to me to make a deal with them to obtain for the young lover the liberty to speak continually with his mistress until Le Havre. I signaled the chief to approach, and I made him the proposal. He appeared ashamed, despite his impudence. "It is not, sir," he replied, looking embarrassed, "that we refuse to let him speak to this girl, but he wishes to be with her constantly; that is inconvenient for us; it is only fair that he pay for the inconvenience." "Let us see, then," I said, "what it would take to prevent you from feeling inconvenienced." He had the audacity to ask me for two louis. I gave them to him on the spot: "But be careful," I told him, "that no trickery escapes you; for I will leave my address with this young man, so that he can inform me, and rest assured that I will have the power to have you punished." It cost me six gold louis. The good grace and lively gratitude with which this young stranger thanked me, convinced me that he was born for something, and that he deserved my liberality. I said a few words to his mistress before leaving. She replied to me with a modesty so gentle and charming that I could not help, upon leaving, making a thousand reflections on the incomprehensible character of women.

11.translation: Returning to my solitude, I was not informed of the continuation of this adventure. Nearly two years passed, during which I forgot it entirely, until chance gave me the opportunity to learn all the circumstances thoroughly. I was arriving from London in Calais, with the Marquis de..., my pupil. We lodged, if I remember correctly, at the Lion d'Or, where certain reasons obliged us to spend the entire day and the following night. Walking in the afternoon in the streets, I thought I recognized the same young man I had met in Pacy. He was in very poor condition and much paler than I had seen him the first time. He was carrying an old valise on his arm, having just arrived in the city. However, as his features were too handsome not to be easily recognized, I immediately placed him. We must, I said to the Marquis, approach this young man. His joy was more lively than any expression, when he in turn recognized me. Ah! sir, he cried, kissing my hand, can I once again express my immortal gratitude to you! I asked him where he was coming from. He replied that he had arrived by sea from Le Havre-de-Grâce, where he had returned from America shortly before. You do not seem to be very well-off financially, I said to him.

12.translation: Go to the Lion d'Or, where I am lodged. I will join you in a moment. I returned there indeed, full of impatience to learn the details of his misfortune and the circumstances of his journey to America. I gave him a thousand caresses, and ordered that he should want for nothing. He did not wait for me to urge him to tell me the story of his life. "Sir," he said to me, "you treat me so nobly, that I would reproach myself, as a base ingratitude, for keeping anything back from you. I wish to tell you, not only my misfortunes and my sorrows, but also my disorders and my most shameful weaknesses. I am sure that in condemning me, you will not be able to avoid pitying me." I must warn the reader here that I wrote his story almost as soon as I heard it, and that one can be assured, consequently, that nothing is more exact and more faithful than this narration. I say faithful even in the account of the reflections and sentiments that the young adventurer expressed with the utmost grace. Here then is his account, to which I will add nothing, until the end, that is not from him. I was seventeen years old, and I was finishing my philosophy studies in Amiens, where my parents, who are from one of the best families in P., had sent me. I led such a wise and regulated life that my teachers held me up as an example for the college."

13.translation: Not that I made extraordinary efforts to deserve this praise, but I have a naturally gentle and tranquil disposition: I applied myself to my studies out of inclination, and some natural signs of aversion to vice were counted as virtues in me. My birth, the success of my studies, and some external pleasantries had made me known and esteemed by all the respectable people in the city. I completed my public exercises with such general approval that Monsieur the Bishop, who was present, proposed that I enter the ecclesiastical state, where, he said, I would not fail to gain more distinction than in the order of Malta, to which my parents were alreadyDestining me. They were already having me wear the cross, with the name Chevalier des Grieux. As the holidays approached, I was preparing to return to my father, who had promised to send me to the Academy soon. My only regret in leaving Amiens was leaving behind a friend with whom I had always been tenderly united. He was a few years older than me. We had been raised together, but the fortune of his family being very modest, he was obliged to enter the ecclesiastical state and to remain in Amiens after me, to pursue the studies suitable for that profession. He possessed a thousand good qualities. You will come to know him through the best of them later in my story, and especially through a zeal and generosity in friendship that surpass the most famous examples of antiquity.

14.dayTitle: A Fateful Encounter at the Inn
14.translation: If I had only followed his advice then, I would have always been wise and happy. If I had, at least, benefited from his rebukes in the precipice where my passions dragged me, I would have salvaged something from the wreck of my fortune and reputation. But he gathered no other fruit from his efforts than the sorrow of seeing them useless and, sometimes, harshly rewarded by an ingrate who took offense at them, and who treated them as importunities. I had set the date for my departure from Amiens. Alas! why didn't I set it one day earlier! I would have brought all my innocence back to my father. The very eve of the day I was to leave this city, while walking with my friend, whose name was Tiberge, we saw the coach from Arras arrive, and we followed it to the inn where these coaches stop. We had no other motive than curiosity. Some women got out, who immediately left. But one remained, very young, who stood alone in the courtyard while an advanced-aged man, who appeared to be her conductor, hurried to have her baggage taken from the baskets. She seemed so charming to me that I, who had never thought about the difference between the sexes, nor looked at a girl with any attention, I, I say, whose wisdom and restraint everyone admired, found myself suddenly inflamed to the point of transport.

15.translation: I had the flaw of being excessively shy and easily disconcerted; but far from being stopped by this weakness, I advanced towards the mistress of my heart. Although she was even younger than I, she received my courtesies without appearing embarrassed. I asked her what brought her to Amiens and if she had any acquaintances there. She ingenuously replied that she had been sent there by her parents to become a nun. Love already made me so enlightened, since it had been in my heart for a moment, that I saw this plan as a fatal blow to my desires. I spoke to her in a way that made her understand my feelings, for she was much more experienced than I. It was against her will that she was being sent to the convent, doubtless to curb her inclination for pleasure, which had already declared itself and which caused, subsequently, all her misfortunes and mine. I fought the cruel intention of her parents with all the reasons my nascent love and my scholastic eloquence could suggest. She affected neither severity nor disdain. She told me, after a moment of silence, that she foresaw only too well that she was going to be unhappy, but that it was apparently the will of Heaven, since it left her no means to avoid it. The sweetness of her gaze, a charming air of sadness as she spoke these words, or rather, the ascendancy of my destiny which was drawing me to my ruin, did not allow me to hesitate for a moment in my reply.

16.translation: I assured her that if she would place some trust in my honor and in the infinite tenderness she already inspired in me, I would spend my life delivering her from the tyranny of her parents and making her happy. I have been astonished a thousand times, reflecting on it, whence came to me then so much boldness and ease of expression; but one would not make a divinity of love if it did not often work wonders. I added a thousand pressing entreaties. My beautiful stranger knew well that one is not deceitful at my age; she confessed to me that if I saw any way to set her free one day, she would consider herself indebted to me for something dearer than life. I repeated to her that I was ready to undertake anything, but, not having enough experience to conceive all at once the means to help her, I confined myself to this general assurance, which could be of little help to her and to me. Her old Argus having come to rejoin us, my hopes were about to fail if she had not had enough wit to compensate for the sterility of mine. I was surprised, upon the arrival of her conductor, that she called me her cousin and that, without appearing the least disconcerted, she told me that, since she was fortunate enough to meet me in Amiens, she postponed her entry into the convent until the next day, in order to procure for herself the pleasure of supping with me.

17.dayTitle: A Cunning Plan and Newfound Feelings
17.translation: I fully understood the meaning of this ruse. I proposed that she lodge in an inn, whose proprietor, who had settled in Amiens after being my father's coachman for a long time, was entirely devoted to my commands. I led her there myself, while the old driver seemed to murmur a little and my friend Tiberge, who understood nothing of this scene, followed me without uttering a word. He had not heard our conversation. He had remained walking in the courtyard while I spoke of love to my beautiful mistress. As I dreaded his wisdom, I got rid of him by asking him to undertake a commission. Thus, upon arriving at the inn, I had the pleasure of conversing alone with the sovereign of my heart. I soon realized that I was less of a child than I thought. My heart opened to a thousand feelings of pleasure that I had never conceived of. A gentle warmth spread through all my veins. I was in a kind of transport that, for some time, deprived me of the freedom of my voice and expressed itself only through my eyes. Mademoiselle Manon Lescaut, as she told me her name was, seemed very pleased with this effect of her charms. I thought I perceived that she was no less moved than I was. She confessed to me that she found me amiable and that she would be delighted to owe her freedom to me.

18.translation: She wanted to know who I was, and this knowledge increased her affection, because, being of common birth, she found herself flattered to have won the love of a man like me. We discussed the means by which we could be together. After many reflections, we found no other way than that of flight. It was necessary to deceive the vigilance of the driver, who, though merely a servant, was a man to be reckoned with. We agreed that I would arrange for a post-chaise to be prepared during the night, and that I would return to the inn early in the morning before he awoke; that we would slip away secretly, and go straight to Paris, where we would get married upon arrival. I had about fifty écus, which were the fruit of my small savings; she had about double that amount. We imagined, like inexperienced children, that this sum would never run out, and we counted no less on the success of our other plans. After supper, with more satisfaction than I had ever felt, I retired to execute our plan. My arrangements were all the easier because, having intended to return to my father's house the next day, my small luggage was already prepared. I therefore had no difficulty in having my trunk transported and a chaise ready by five in the morning, which was when the city gates were to be opened; but I encountered an obstacle I did not at all suspect, and which almost completely ruined my plan.

vocabulary.translation: to steal away, to slip away

19.translation: Tiberge, though only three years older than I, was a young man of mature sense and very regular conduct. He loved me with extraordinary tenderness. The sight of a girl as pretty as Mademoiselle Manon, my eagerness to escort her, and the care I had taken to get rid of him by sending him away, gave rise to some suspicions of my love. He had not dared to return to the inn where he had left me, for fear of offending me by his return; but he had gone to wait for me at my lodging, where I found him upon arriving, although it was ten o'clock at night. His presence distressed me. He easily noticed the constraint it caused me. "I am sure," he said to me without disguise, "that you are contemplating some plan which you wish to hide from me; I see it in your demeanor." I replied rather abruptly that I was not obliged to account to him for all my plans. "No," he replied, "but you have always treated me as a friend, and that quality implies a certain degree of trust and openness." He pressed me so hard and for so long to reveal my secret that, having never had any reserve with him, I made him a full confession of my passion. He received it with an appearance of displeasure that made me shudder. I especially repented the indiscretion with which I had revealed to him my plan to flee.

20.translation: He told me that he was too much my perfect friend to not oppose it with all his power; that he wanted first to represent to me everything he believed capable of dissuading me from it, but that, if I did not then renounce this miserable resolution, he would inform people who could surely stop me. He gave me a serious lecture on this subject that lasted more than a quarter of an hour, and which ended once again with the threat of denouncing me if I did not give him my word to conduct myself with more wisdom and reason. I was in despair at having betrayed myself so inopportunely. However, love having greatly opened my mind in the last two or three hours, I noticed that I had not revealed to him that my plan was to be executed the next day, and I resolved to deceive him by means of an ambiguity: Tiberge, I said to him, I believed until now that you were my friend, and I wanted to test you with this confidence, it is true that I love, I have not deceived you, but, as for my flight, it is not an undertaking to be formed by chance. Come and get me tomorrow at nine o'clock, I will show you, if possible, my mistress, and you will judge if she deserves that I take this step for her. He left me alone, after a thousand protestations of friendship.

21.translation: I spent the night putting my affairs in order, and upon reaching Mademoiselle Manon's inn towards dawn, I found her waiting for me. She was at her window, which overlooked the street, so that, having spotted me, she came to open the door herself. We left without a sound. She had no other luggage than her linen, which I took charge of myself. The carriage was ready to leave; we immediately distanced ourselves from the town. I will recount later what Tiberge's conduct was when he realized I had deceived him. His zeal did not become any less ardent. You will see to what extreme he carried it, and how much I should shed tears thinking that its reward has always been such. We hurried our advance so much that we arrived at Saint-Denis before nightfall. I had ridden on horseback beside the carriage, which had hardly allowed us to converse except when changing horses; but when we saw ourselves so close to Paris, that is to say, almost safe, we took the time to refresh ourselves, having eaten nothing since our departure from Amiens. However passionate I was for Manon, she knew how to persuade me that she was no less so for me. We were so unrestrained in our caresses that we did not have the patience to wait until we were alone.

22.dayTitle: A Furtive Union and Fleeting Happiness
22.translation: Our postillions and hosts looked at us with admiration, and I noticed they were surprised to see two children of our age, who seemed to love each other to the point of fury. Our marriage plans were forgotten at Saint-Denis; we defrauded the Church's rights, and found ourselves married without having given it a thought. It is certain that, with the tender and constant nature I possess, I would have been happy for all my life if Manon had been faithful to me. The more I knew her, the more I discovered in her new amiable qualities. Her wit, her heart, her gentleness, and her beauty formed a chain so strong and so charming that I would have found all my happiness in never leaving it. Terrible change! What causes my despair could have brought me my felicity. I find myself the unhappiest of all men, because of that very constancy from which I should have expected the sweetest of all fates and the most perfect rewards of love. We took a furnished apartment in Paris. It was on V... street, and, to my misfortune, near the house of M. de B..., a famous tax collector. Three weeks passed, during which I had been so filled with my passion that I had thought little of my family and the grief my father must have felt at my absence. However, as debauchery played no part in my conduct, and as Manon also behaved with great restraint, the tranquility in which we lived served to gradually bring back to my mind the idea of my duty.

vocabulary.translation: to think, to dream (here: to have thought)

23.dayTitle: Des Grieux Plans Reconciliation and Marriage
23.translation: I resolved to reconcile myself, if possible, with my father. My mistress was so amiable that I did not doubt she could please him, if I found a way to make him know her wisdom and merit: in a word, I flattered myself that I could obtain his permission to marry her, having been disillusioned of the hope of being able to do so without his consent. I shared this plan with Manon, and I made her understand that besides the motives of love and duty, that of necessity might also play a part, for our funds were extremely depleted, and I was beginning to abandon the opinion that they were inexhaustible. Manon received this proposal coolly. However, the difficulties she raised were based solely on her tenderness itself and the fear of losing me, if my father did not approve of our plan after learning the location of our retreat, so I did not have the slightest suspicion of the cruel blow that was being prepared for me. To the objection of necessity, she replied that we still had enough to live on for a few weeks, and that after that, she would find resources in the affection of some relatives to whom she would write in the provinces. She softened her refusal with caresses so tender and passionate that I, who lived only for her and who had not the slightest distrust of her heart, applauded all her answers and all her resolutions.

24.translation: I had left the management of our purse to her, and the care of paying our ordinary expenses. I noticed, shortly after, that our table was better served, and that she had made some rather expensive adjustments to her attire. As I knew that we had barely twelve or fifteen pistoles left, I expressed my astonishment at this apparent increase in our wealth. She asked me, laughing, not to worry. Hadn't I promised her, she said, that I would find resources? I loved her with too much simplicity to be easily alarmed. One day, when I had gone out in the afternoon, and had told her I would be out longer than usual, I was surprised that upon my return I was made to wait two or three minutes at the door. We were only served by a young maid who was about our age. When she came to open the door for me, I asked her why she had delayed so long. She replied, with an embarrassed air, that she had not heard me knock. I had only knocked once; I said to her: but, if you didn't hear me, why did you come to open the door? This question disconcerted her so much that, not having enough presence of mind to answer, she began to cry, assuring me that it was not her fault, and that Madam had forbidden her to open the door until Mr.

25.translation: de B... had left by the other staircase which led to the study. I remained so confused that I did not have the strength to enter the apartment. I decided to go down under the pretext of an errand, and I instructed the child to tell his mistress that I would return shortly, but not to let it be known that she had spoken to me of Mr. de B... My consternation was so great that I shed tears as I descended the stairs, without yet knowing what feeling prompted them. I entered the first café and, having sat down near a table, I rested my head on my hands to unravel what was happening in my heart. I dared not recall what I had just heard. I wanted to consider it an illusion, and I was ready two or three times to return home without showing that I had paid attention. It seemed so impossible to me that Manon had betrayed me that I feared doing her an injury by suspecting her. I adored her, that was certain; I had not given her more proof of love than I had received from her; why should I have accused her of being less sincere and less constant than me? What reason would she have had to deceive me? It had only been three hours since she had overwhelmed me with her most tender caresses and had received mine with transport; I knew my heart no better than hers.

26.translation: No, no, I replied, it is not possible that Manon would betray me. She is not unaware that I live only for her. She knows too well that I adore her. That is not a reason to hate me. However, the visit and the furtive departure of M. de B... caused me embarrassment. I also recalled Manon's little acquisitions, which seemed to exceed our present wealth. This appeared to hint at the generosity of a new lover. And that confidence she had shown me regarding resources unknown to me! I could hardly give such enigmas a meaning as favorable as my heart wished. On the other hand, I had hardly lost sight of her since we had been in Paris. Occupations, walks, entertainments, we had always been next to each other; my God! a moment of separation would have afflicted us too greatly. We had to tell each other constantly that we loved each other; we would have died of worry otherwise. I therefore could hardly imagine a single moment when Manon could have been occupied with anyone other than me. In the end, I thought I had found the solution to this mystery. M. de B..., I said to myself, is a man who does big business and has great connections; Manon's parents will have used this man to have some money sent to her.

vocabulary.translation: to be unaware of, to ignore

27.dayTitle: Manon's Secret Gift and Somber Mood
27.translation: She may have already received some from him; he came today to bring her more. She undoubtedly made a game of hiding it from me, to surprise me pleasantly. Perhaps she would have told me about it if I had returned as usual, instead of coming here to grieve me; she will not hide it from me, at least, when I speak to her about it myself. I became so strongly convinced of this idea that it had the strength to greatly lessen my sadness. I returned home immediately. I embraced Manon with my usual tenderness. She received me very well. I was at first tempted to reveal my conjectures to her, which I considered more certain than ever; I restrained myself, in the hope that she might perhaps forestall me by telling me all that had happened. We were served supper. I sat down at the table with a very cheerful air; but in the candlelight, which was between her and me, I thought I perceived sadness on the face and in the eyes of my dear mistress. This thought inspired some in me as well. I noticed that her gaze was fixed on me in a different way than usual. I could not discern whether it was love or compassion, although it seemed to me to be a soft and languishing sentiment. I looked at her with the same attention; and perhaps she had no less difficulty judging the state of my heart from my gaze.

28.translation: We thought neither of speaking nor of eating. Finally, I saw tears fall from her beautiful eyes: treacherous tears! Ah Gods! I cried out, you are weeping, my dear Manon; you are so distressed as to weep, and you do not speak a single word of your troubles to me. She answered me only with a few sighs which increased my anxiety. I rose, trembling. I implored her, with all the urgency of love, to reveal the cause of her tears; I shed some myself while wiping hers; I was more dead than alive. A savage would have been moved by the testimonies of my pain and my fear. At the time when I was thus entirely occupied with her, I heard the noise of several people ascending the stairs. There was a gentle knock at the door. Manon gave me a kiss and, escaping from my arms, she quickly entered the closet, which she immediately locked behind her. I imagined that, being a little dishevelled, she wanted to hide from the eyes of the strangers who had knocked. I went to open it myself. No sooner had I opened it, than I found myself seized by three men, whom I recognized as my father's lackeys. They did me no violence; but two of them having taken me by the arm, the third searched my pockets, from which he drew a small knife, which was the only metal I had on me.

29.dayTitle: Despair and Betrayal on the Road
29.translation: They asked my pardon for the necessity they were under of disrespecting me; they told me naturally that they acted by my father's order, and that my elder brother was waiting for me downstairs in a carriage. I was so upset that I allowed myself to be led away without resisting or replying. My brother was indeed waiting for me. I was put into the carriage beside him, and the coachman, who had his orders, drove us at great speed to Saint-Denis. My brother embraced me tenderly, but he did not speak to me, so that I had all the leisure I needed to reflect on my misfortune. At first, I found so much obscurity in it that I could not see the slightest glimmer of hope. I had been cruelly betrayed. But by whom? Tiberge was the first who came to my mind. Traitor! I said to myself, your life is forfeit if my suspicions prove true. However, I reflected that he did not know where I lived, and that consequently, he could not have learned it from him. Accusing Manon was something my heart dared not be guilty of. That extraordinary sadness with which I had seen her as if overwhelmed, her tears, the tender kiss she had given me upon leaving, seemed indeed an enigma to me; but I felt inclined to explain it as a premonition of our shared misfortune, and at the time I despaired of the accident that tore me away from her, I had the credulity to imagine that she was even more to be pitied than I.

30.translation: The result of my reflection was to convince me that I had been seen in the streets of Paris by some acquaintances, who had informed my father. This thought consoled me. I expected to get off with reprimands or some ill-treatment, which I would have to endure from paternal authority. I resolved to suffer them patiently, and to promise everything that would be demanded of me, in order to facilitate my return to Paris more promptly, and to go and bring life and joy back to my dear Manon. We arrived, in a short time, at Saint-Denis. My brother, surprised by my silence, imagined it was due to my fear. He undertook to console me by assuring me that I had nothing to fear from my father's severity, provided that I was willing to return gently to my duty and to earn the affection he had for me. He made me spend the night at Saint-Denis, taking the precaution of having the three lackeys sleep in my room. What caused me keen distress was to find myself in the same inn where I had stopped with Manon, when coming from Amiens to Paris. The innkeeper and the servants recognized me, and at the same time guessed the truth of my story. I heard the innkeeper say: Ah!

31.translation: This is that handsome gentleman who passed by six weeks ago with a young lady he loved so much. How charming she was! The poor children, how they caressed each other! Pardi, it's a shame they were separated. I pretended not to hear anything, and I made myself as invisible as possible. My brother had a two-seater carriage in Saint-Denis, in which we set off early in the morning, and we arrived home the following evening. He saw my father before me, to speak in my favor by telling him how gently I had allowed myself to be led, so that I was received less harshly than I had expected. He contented himself with making me some general reproaches about the fault I had committed in leaving without his permission. As for my mistress, he told me that I deserved what had happened to me by giving myself to a stranger; that he had had a better opinion of my prudence, but that he hoped this little adventure would make me wiser. I took this speech only in the sense that agreed with my ideas. I thanked my father for his kindness in forgiving me, and I promised to behave more submissively and more regularly. I triumphed in my heart, because of the way things were arranged, I did not doubt at all that I would have the freedom to slip away from home, even before the end of the night.

vocabulary.translation: to steal away, to slip away

32.translation: We sat down to supper; they mocked me about my conquest of Amiens, and about my flight with that faithful mistress. I took the teasing in good grace. I was even pleased to be allowed to speak of what was constantly on my mind. But, a few words dropped by my father made me listen with the utmost attention: he spoke of perfidy and self-interested service rendered by Monsieur B... I remained dumbfounded hearing him pronounce that name, and I humbly asked him to explain further. He turned to my brother, asking him if he hadn't told me the whole story. My brother replied that I had seemed so calm on the road, that he hadn't thought I needed this remedy to cure my madness. I noticed that my father hesitated whether to finish explaining. I urged him so insistently, that he satisfied me, or rather, that he cruelly assassinated me with the most terrible of all stories. He first asked me if I had always been so naive as to believe that I was loved by my mistress. I boldly told him that I was so sure of it that nothing could make me doubt it in the least. Ha! ha! ha! he exclaimed, laughing with all his might, that's excellent! You are a fine dupe, and I like to see you in such sentiments.

33.translation: It is a great pity, my poor Chevalier, to make you enter the Order of Malta, since you have such a disposition for being a patient and convenient husband. He added a thousand jests of this nature, concerning what he called my foolishness and credulity. Finally, as I remained silent, he continued to tell me that, by his calculation of the time since my departure from Amiens, Manon had loved me for about twelve days: for, he added, I know you left Amiens on the 28th of last month; we are on the 29th of this one; Monsieur B... wrote to me eleven days ago; I suppose it took him eight to form a perfect acquaintance with your mistress; thus, if you subtract eleven and eight from the thirty-one days between the 28th of one month and the 29th of the next, twelve remain, more or less. Thereupon, peals of laughter began again. I listened to everything with a seizing of the heart that I feared I could not resist until the end of this sad comedy. You will learn then, my father resumed, since you are ignorant of it, that Monsieur B... has won the heart of your princess, for he mocks me by pretending to persuade me that it is out of disinterested zeal for my service that he wished to take her from you. It is indeed from a man like him, whom, moreover, I do not know, that one should expect such noble sentiments!

34.translation: He learned from her that you are my son, and to be rid of your importunities, he wrote to me the place of your dwelling and the disorder in which you were living, making me understand that force was needed to secure you. He offered to facilitate the means for me to seize you by the collar, and it was through his direction and that of your mistress that your brother found the moment to take you unawares. Rejoice now in the duration of your triumph. You know how to conquer quickly enough, Chevalier; but you do not know how to keep your conquests. I did not have the strength to support any longer a discourse whose every word had pierced my heart. I rose from the table, and I had not taken four steps to leave the room, when I fell to the floor senseless and unconscious. They brought me back to my senses with their prompt aid. I opened my eyes to shed a torrent of tears, and my mouth to utter the saddest and most touching complaints. My father, who has always loved me tenderly, employed all his affection to console me. I listened to him, but without hearing him. I threw myself at his knees, I beseeched him, joining my hands, to let me return to Paris to go and stab B... No, I said, he has not won Manon's heart, he has forced her; he has seduced her by a charm or by a poison; he may have brutally forced her.

35.translation: Manon loves me. Do I not know this well? He must have threatened her, dagger in hand, to force her to abandon me. What wouldn't he have done to snatch such a charming mistress from me! Oh gods! gods! Could it be possible that Manon had betrayed me, and that she had stopped loving me! As I kept talking about returning quickly to Paris, and even getting up at all moments for that, my father saw well that, in the state I was in, nothing would be able to stop me. He led me to a high room, where he left two servants with me to guard me. I was not in control of myself. I would have given a thousand lives just to be a quarter of an hour in Paris. I understood that, having declared myself so openly, they would not easily allow me to leave my room. I measured with my eyes the height of the windows, seeing no possibility of escaping that way, I addressed my two servants gently. I promised them, with a thousand oaths, to make their fortune one day, if they wanted to consent to my escape. I pressed them, I caressed them, I threatened them; but this attempt was also useless. I then lost all hope. I resolved to die, and I threw myself on a bed with the intention of not leaving it except with my life. I spent the night and the following day in this situation.

36.translation: I refused the food that was brought to me the next day. My father came to see me in the afternoon. He kindly sought to console my sorrows with the sweetest consolations. He absolutely ordered me to eat something, so I did out of respect for his orders. A few days passed, during which I ate nothing except in his presence and to obey him. He continued to bring me reasons that could bring me back to my senses and inspire me with contempt for the unfaithful Manon. It is certain that I no longer esteemed her; how could I esteem the most fickle and most perfidious of all creatures? But her image, her charming features that I carried deep in my heart, remained there always. I felt this well. I may die, I said; I should even, after so much shame and pain; but I would suffer a thousand deaths without being able to forget the ungrateful Manon. My father was surprised to see me still so deeply affected. He knew my principles of honor, and not doubting that her betrayal would make me despise her, he imagined that my constancy came less from this particular passion than from a general inclination toward women. He became so attached to this thought that, consulting only his tender affection, he came one day to speak to me about it. Knight, he said to me, I had intended, until now, to have you wear the cross of Malta, but I see that your inclinations are not turned in that direction.

37.translation: You like beautiful women. I advise you to find one that pleases you. Explain to me naturally what you think about this. I replied that I no longer made any distinction between women, and that after the misfortune that had just happened to me, I hated them all equally. I will find one for you, my father replied with a smile, who will resemble Manon, and who will be more faithful. Ah! if you have any kindness for me, I said to him, it is her that you must return to me. Be sure, my dear father, that she has not betrayed me; she is not capable of such a black and cruel cowardice. It is the perfidious B... who deceives us, you, her and me. If you knew how tender and sincere she is, if you knew her, you would love her yourself. You are a child, my father replied. How can you blind yourself to this extent, after what I have told you about her? It was she herself who delivered you to your brother. You should forget even her name, and if you are wise, take advantage of the indulgence I have for you. I recognized too clearly that he was right. It was an involuntary movement that made me take the side of my unfaithful one. Alas! I replied, after a moment of silence, it is only too true that I am the unhappy object of the most cowardly of all perfidies.

38.translation: Yes," I continued, shedding tears of frustration, "I see well enough that I am nothing but a child. My credulity cost them little to deceive me. But I know well what I must do to take my revenge. My father wished to know what my plan was. 'I will go to Paris,' I told him, 'I will set fire to B...'s house, and I will burn him alive along with the perfidious Manon.' This outburst made my father laugh and served only to have me kept more closely confined in my prison. I spent six entire months there, during the first of which there was little change in my disposition. All my feelings were nothing but a perpetual alternation of hatred and love, of hope or despair, according to the image under which Manon presented herself to my mind. At one moment I saw in her nothing but the most charming of all girls, and I languished with the desire to see her again; at another moment I perceived in her nothing but a cowardly and perfidious mistress, and I made a thousand oaths to seek her only to punish her. They gave me books, which served to bring a little tranquility to my soul. I reread all my authors; I acquired new knowledge; I regained an infinite taste for study. You will see how useful it was to me later. The enlightenment I owed to love made me find clarity in many passages of Horace and Virgil that had seemed obscure to me before.

39.translation: I wrote a loving commentary on the fourth book of the Aeneid; I intend for it to be published and I flatter myself that the public will be satisfied with it. Alas! I said as I was writing it, it was a heart like mine that the faithful Dido needed. Tiberge came to visit me one day in my prison. I was surprised by the passion with which he embraced me. I had not yet had any proof of his affection that could make me view it otherwise than as a simple college friendship, such as forms between young men who are of approximately the same age. I found him so changed and so mature, since five or six months had passed since I had last seen him, that his face and the tone of his speech inspired respect in me. He spoke to me as a wise counselor, rather than as a school friend. He lamented the error into which I had fallen. He congratulated me on my recovery, which he believed was progressing; finally, he urged me to profit from this youthful error to open my eyes to the vanity of pleasures. I looked at him with astonishment. He noticed it. My dear Knight said to me, I am telling you nothing that is not solidly true, and of which I have not convinced myself through serious examination. I had as much inclination as you toward voluptuousness, but Heaven had given me at the same time a taste for virtue.

40.translation: I used my reason to compare the fruits of each path, and I did not long to discover their differences. The help of Heaven joined my reflections. I conceived for the world a contempt to which there is nothing equal. Would you guess what keeps me here, he added, and what prevents me from rushing to solitude? It is solely the tender friendship I have for you. I know the excellence of your heart and mind; there is nothing good that you could not make yourself capable of. The poison of pleasure has made you stray from the path. What a loss for virtue! Your flight from Amiens caused me so much pain, that I have not tasted a single moment of satisfaction since. Judge for yourself by the steps it made me take. He told me that after realizing that I had deceived him and that I had left with my mistress, he had mounted his horse to follow me; but having four or five hours' head start, it had been impossible for him to catch up with me; that he had nevertheless arrived at Saint-Denis half an hour after my departure; that being quite certain that I would have stopped in Paris, he had spent six weeks there searching for me in vain; that he went to all the places where he flattered himself he might find me, and that one day he finally recognized my mistress at the theater; that she was there in such brilliant attire that he imagined she must owe this fortune to a new lover; that he had followed her carriage to her house, and that he had learned from a servant that she was maintained by the generosity of Monsieur B...

41.translation: I didn't stop there, he continued. I went back the next day to learn from her what had become of you; she suddenly left when she heard me speak of you, and I had to return to the province without any further clarification. There I learned of your adventure and the extreme consternation it has caused you; but I didn't want to see you without being sure of finding you more calm. So you have seen Manon, I replied with a sigh. Alas! You are happier than me, who am condemned never to see her again. He reproached me for that sigh which still showed weakness for her. He flattered me so skillfully on the goodness of my character and my inclinations, that he made me develop, from that first visit, a strong desire to renounce like him all the pleasures of the world to enter the ecclesiastical state. I tasted this idea so much that, when I found myself alone, I didn't occupy myself with anything else. I remembered the words of the Bishop of Amiens, who had given me the same advice, and the happy omens he had formed in my favor, if I were to embrace this decision. Piety also mixed into my considerations. I will lead a wise and Christian life, I said; I will occupy myself with study and religion, which will not allow me to think of the dangerous pleasures of love.

42.dayTitle: My Ideal Life, Manon, and Father's Approval
42.translation: I shall scorn what the common run of men admire; and as I am well aware that my heart will desire only what it esteems, I shall have as few worries as I have desires. On this basis, I had already formulated a system for a peaceful and solitary life. It included a secluded house, with a small wood and a freshwater stream at the end of the garden, a library composed of chosen books, a small number of virtuous and sensible friends, a neat table, but frugal and moderate. To this, I added a correspondence with a friend residing in Paris, who would inform me of public news, less to satisfy my curiosity than to entertain me with the foolish agitations of men. Shall I not be happy? I added; will not all my aspirations be fulfilled? It is certain that this project greatly flattered my inclinations. But, at the end of such a wise arrangement, I felt that my heart still awaited something, and that, to have nothing left to desire in the most charming solitude, Manon had to be there with me. Meanwhile, Tiberge continued to pay me frequent visits, in line with the purpose he had inspired in me, and I took the opportunity to broach the subject with my father. He declared to me that his intention was to leave his children free in the choice of their condition, and that, in whatever manner I wished to dispose of myself, he would reserve only the right to assist me with his counsel.

43.translation: He gave me very wise advice, which tended less to discourage me from my project than to make me embrace it with full understanding. The renewal of the scholastic year was approaching. I agreed with Tiberge to enter the Saint-Sulpice seminary together, he to complete his theological studies, and I to begin mine. His merit, which was known to the bishop of the diocese, enabled him to obtain a considerable benefice from this prelate before our departure. My father, believing me entirely recovered from my passion, made no difficulty in letting me leave. We arrived in Paris. The ecclesiastical habit replaced the cross of Malta, and the name Abbé des Grieux replaced that of Chevalier. I applied myself to study with such diligence that I made extraordinary progress in a few months. I devoted a part of the night to it, and I did not lose a moment of the day. My reputation shone so brightly that I was already being congratulated on the dignities I could not fail to obtain, and without having solicited it, my name was put on the list of benefices. Piety was no less neglected; I had fervor for all exercises. Tiberge was charmed by what he regarded as his work, and I saw him shed tears several times, applauding himself for what he called my conversion.

44.translation: That human resolutions are subject to change is something that has never surprised me; a passion gives birth to them, another passion can destroy them; but when I think about the holiness of those that had led me to Saint-Sulpice and the inner joy that Heaven made me taste there while carrying them out, I am frightened by the ease with which I was able to break them. If it is true that heavenly aids are at all times equal in strength to the passions, then explain to me by what fatal influence one finds himself suddenly carried away from his duty without being capable of the least resistance, and without feeling the least remorse. I believed myself absolutely delivered from the weaknesses of love. It seemed to me that I would have preferred reading a page of Saint Augustine, or a quarter-hour of Christian meditation, to all the pleasures of the senses, without exception those that would have been offered to me by Manon. However, an unfortunate moment made me fall back into the precipice, and my fall was all the more irreparable that, finding myself suddenly at the same depth from which I had emerged, the new disorders into which I fell carried me much further toward the bottom of the abyss. I had spent nearly a year in Paris, without inquiring about Manon's affairs.

45.translation: It had cost me a great deal at first to make this violence to myself; but the ever-present advice of Tiberge, and my own reflections, had helped me obtain victory. The last few months had passed so tranquilly that I believed I was about to forget forever this charming and treacherous creature. The time came when I had to sustain a public exercise in the School of Theology. I had several people of consideration asked to honor me with their presence. My name was thus spread throughout all quarters of Paris: it reached even the ears of my unfaithful one. She did not recognize it with certainty under the title of abbot; but a remnant of curiosity, or perhaps some regret for having betrayed me—I have never been able to disentangle which of these two feelings made her take interest in a name so similar to mine; she came to the Sorbonne with some other ladies. She was present at my exercise, and without doubt she had little trouble in recognizing me. I had no knowledge of this visit. One knows that there are, in these places, private cabinets for ladies, where they are hidden behind a jalousie (latticed screen). I returned to Saint-Sulpice, covered with glory and loaded with compliments. It was six o'clock in the evening. A moment after my return, I was informed that a lady was asking to see me. I went to the parlor at once.

46.translation: Gods! what a surprising apparition! I found Manon there. It was her, but more charming and brilliant than I had ever seen her. She was in her eighteenth year. Her charms surpassed everything that can be described. It was such a fine, sweet, engaging air, the air of Love itself. Her whole face seemed to me an enchantment. I remained dumbfounded at her sight, and not being able to conjecture what was the purpose of this visit, I waited, with eyes lowered and trembling, for her to explain herself. Her embarrassment was, for some time, equal to mine, but, seeing that my silence continued, she put her hand before her eyes, to hide some tears. She said to me, in a timid tone, that she confessed that her infidelity deserved my hatred; but that, if it were true that I had ever had some tenderness for her, there had also been great hardness in letting two years pass without taking care to inform myself of her fate, and that there was still much more in seeing her in the state she was in my presence, without saying a word to her. The disorder of my soul, in listening to her, cannot be expressed. She sat down. I remained standing, half-turned, not daring to look at her directly. I began several times a response, which I did not have the strength to finish. Finally, I made an effort to cry out painfully: Treacherous Manon!

47.translation: Ah! perfidious! perfidious! She repeated to me, weeping hot tears, that she did not intend to justify her perfidy. What do you intend then? I cried out again. I intend to die, she replied, if you do not return your heart to me, without which it is impossible for me to live. Then demand my life, unfaithful! I retorted, shedding tears myself which I tried in vain to hold back. Demand my life, which is the only thing I have left to sacrifice to you; for my heart has never ceased to be yours. Scarcely had I finished these last words, when she rose with transport to come embrace me. She overwhelmed me with a thousand passionate caresses. She called me by all the names that love invents to express its most vivid tenderness. I still only answered with languor. What a passage, indeed, from the tranquil situation in which I had been, to the tumultuous feelings that I felt reawakening! I was terrified by it. I shuddered, as happens when one finds oneself at night in a remote countryside: one believes oneself transported into a new order of things; one is seized with a secret horror, from which one only recovers after having long considered all the surroundings. We sat down one near the other. I took her hands in mine. Ah! Manon, I said to her, looking at her with a sad eye, I had not expected the black treachery with which you have repaid my love.

48.translation: It was very easy for you to deceive a heart of which you were the absolute sovereign, and which put all its happiness in pleasing and obeying you. Tell me now if you have found others as tender and as submissive. No, no, Nature rarely makes others of the same temper as mine. Tell me, at least, if you have sometimes regretted it. What foundation should I make on this return of goodness that brings you back today to console him? I see only too well that you are more charming than ever; but in the name of all the pains I have suffered for you, beautiful Manon, tell me if you will be more faithful. She answered me with such touching things about her repentance and she engaged herself to fidelity by so many protests and oaths, that she moved me to an inexpressible degree. Dear Manon! I said to her, with a profane mixture of loving and theological expressions, you are too adorable for a creature. I feel my heart carried away by a victorious delight. All that is said about liberty at Saint-Sulpice is a chimera. I am going to lose my fortune and my reputation for you, I foresee it well; I read my destiny in your beautiful eyes; but of what losses would I not be consoled by your love! The favors of fortune do not touch me; glory seems to me smoke; all my projects of an ecclesiastical life were foolish imaginations; finally all goods different from those I hope for with you are despicable goods, since they cannot hold a moment, in my heart against a single one of your glances.

49.translation: Nevertheless, while promising her general forgiveness for her faults, I wanted to know how she had allowed herself to be seduced by B... She told me that, having seen her at her window, he had become passionate for her; that he had made his proposal as a tax farmer, that is to say by marking in a letter that payment would be proportional to her favors; that she had surrendered at first, but with no other intention than to extract from him a considerable sum that could serve to allow us to live comfortably; that he had dazzled her with such magnificent promises that she had gradually been swayed; that I should judge her remorse, however, by the pain she had shown me evidence of the day before our separation; that despite the opulence in which he had kept her, she had never tasted happiness with him, not only because she found there, she said, neither the delicacy of my feelings nor the charm of my manners, but because even amidst the pleasures he constantly procured for her, she carried in the depths of her heart the memory of my love and the remorse of her infidelity. She spoke to me of Tiberge and the extreme embarrassment his visit had caused her. 'A sword thrust to the heart,' she added, 'would have moved my blood less.' I turned my back on her, unable to bear her presence for even a moment.

50.translation: She continued to tell me by what means she had been informed of my stay in Paris, of the change in my circumstances, and of my studies at the Sorbonne. She assured me that she had been so agitated during the argument that she had great difficulty not only in holding back her tears, but even her sobs and cries, which had more than once been on the verge of bursting forth. Finally, she told me that she had been the last to leave that place, to hide her distress, and that, following only the impulse of her heart and the impetuosity of her desires, she had come straight to the seminary, with the resolution to die there if she did not find me willing to forgive her. Where could one find a barbarian whom such a lively and tender repentance would not have touched? As for me, I felt at that moment that I would have sacrificed for Manon all the bishoprics in the Christian world. I asked her what new arrangement she thought proper to make in our affairs. She told me that we needed to leave the seminary immediately, and to make our arrangements in a safer place. I consented to all her wishes without reply. She got into her carriage to wait for me at the corner of the street. I slipped away a moment later without being seen by the porter. I got in with her.

51.translation: We went to the second-hand shop. I reclaimed the braids and the sword. Manon covered the expenses, as I was without a penny; and for fear that I might encounter obstacles in leaving Saint-Sulpice, she had not wanted me to return for a moment to my room to take my money. My treasure moreover was mediocre, and she was quite rich from the generosity of B... to despise what she was making me abandon. We conferred, with the second-hand dealer himself, on the course we were going to take. To make me more appreciate the sacrifice she was making of B... for me, she resolved to show him no consideration whatsoever. 'I'll let him keep his furniture,' she said to me, 'it belongs to him; but I will take, as is right, the jewelry and nearly sixty thousand francs that I have drawn from him these past two years. I have given him no power over me,' she added; 'thus we can remain without fear in Paris, by taking a convenient house where we will live happily.' I pointed out to her that if there was no danger for her, there was much for me, who would not fail sooner or later to be recognized, and who would be continually exposed to the misfortune I had already experienced. She made me understand that she would regret leaving Paris. I feared so much to distress her that there was no risk I wouldn't scorn to please her; however, we found a reasonable compromise, which was to rent a house in some village near Paris, from which it would be easy for us to go to the city when pleasure or need called us there.

52.dayTitle: Manon and I Settle in Chaillot
52.translation: We chose Chaillot, which is not far away. Manon returned immediately to her home. I went to wait for her at the small gate of the Tuileries garden. She returned an hour later, in a hired carriage, with a maid who served her, and some trunks in which her clothes and all her precious belongings were contained. We did not delay in reaching Chaillot. We stayed the first night at the inn, to give ourselves time to look for a house, or at least a convenient apartment. We found one to our liking the very next day. My happiness seemed to me at first to be established in an unshakable way. Manon was sweetness and compliance itself. She had for me such delicate attentions, that I believed myself too perfectly compensated for all my troubles. As we had both acquired a little experience, we reasoned about the solidity of our fortune. Sixty thousand francs, which formed the foundation of our wealth, were not a sum that could stretch as long as the course of a long life. We were not moreover disposed to restrict our expenses too much. The first virtue of Manon, no more than mine, was not economy. Here is the plan I proposed to myself: Sixty thousand francs, I said to her, can support us for ten years. Two thousand crowns will be enough for us each year, if we continue to live in Chaillot.

53.translation: We would lead an honest, but simple life. Our only expenses would be for maintaining a carriage and for the shows. We would regulate ourselves. You love the Opera: we will go twice a week. For gambling, we will limit ourselves so that our losses never exceed two pistoles. It is impossible that within ten years, no change will occur in my family; my father is old, he may die. I will find myself with wealth, and then we will be above all our other fears. This arrangement would not have been the most foolish action of my life, if we had been wise enough to constantly submit to it. But our resolutions lasted hardly more than a month. Manon was passionate about pleasure; I was passionate about her. New occasions for expense arose for us at all times; and far from regretting the sums that she sometimes used with profusion, I was the first to provide her with everything I believed would please her. Our residence in Chaillot began even to become a burden to her. Winter was approaching; everyone was returning to the city, and the countryside was becoming deserted. She proposed to me that we take a house in Paris again. I did not consent to it; but, to satisfy her in some way, I told her that we could rent a furnished apartment there, and that we would spend the night there when it happened that we left the assembly too late, which we attended several times a week, for the inconvenience of returning so late to Chaillot was the pretext that she brought for wanting to leave.

vocabulary.translation: a type of gold coin (historical term)

54.translation: We thus established two residences, one in the city and one in the countryside. This change soon threw our affairs into complete disorder, giving rise to two adventures that led to our ruin. Manon had a brother who was a bodyguard. Unfortunately, he happened to be lodged in Paris on the same street as us. He recognized his sister when he saw her at her window one morning. He immediately came rushing to our home. He was a brutal man without principles of honor. He entered our room swearing horribly, and since he knew some of his sister's adventures, he heaped insults and reproaches upon her. I had gone out a moment before, which was undoubtedly fortunate for him or for me, who was by no means inclined to suffer an insult. I did not return home until after his departure. Manon's sadness led me to judge that something extraordinary had happened. She told me about the unpleasant scene she had just experienced and her brother's brutal threats. I felt so much resentment that I would have immediately rushed to take revenge if she had not stopped me with her tears. While I was discussing this adventure with her, the bodyguard returned to the room where we were, without having been announced. I would not have received him as civilly as I did if I had known him; but, greeting us with a smiling air, he had time to tell Manon that he had come to apologize for his behavior; that he had believed her to be in disarray, and that this opinion had ignited his anger; but that, having learned who I was from one of our servants, he had learned from me such advantageous things that they made him desire to live well with us.

55.translation: Although this information, which came from one of my footmen, had something bizarre and shocking about it, I received his compliment with honesty. I thought I was pleasing Manon. She seemed delighted to see him reconciling. We kept him to dinner. He became, in little moments, so familiar that having heard us talk about our return to Chaillot, he absolutely wanted to keep us company. We had to give him a place in our carriage. It was a taking of possession, for he soon accustomed himself to seeing us with such pleasure that he made our house his own and became, as it were, the master of everything that belonged to us. He called me his brother, and under the pretext of fraternal freedom, he put himself in the position of bringing all his friends to our house in Chaillot, and of entertaining them at our expense. He had himself magnificently dressed at our expense. He even engaged us to pay all his debts. I closed my eyes to this tyranny, so as not to displease Manon, even to feigning not to notice that he was drawing from her, from time to time, considerable sums. It is true, being a great gambler, he had the fidelity to return her a part when fortune favored him; but ours was too mediocre to provide long for such little moderate expenses.

56.translation: I was about to have a strong explanation with him, to free ourselves from his importunities, when a fatal accident spared me this trouble, causing us another that ruined us without recourse. We had stayed a day in Paris, as it happened to us quite often. The servant, who remained alone at Chaillot on these occasions, came to warn me in the morning that fire had broken out during the night in my house, and that people had great difficulty in extinguishing it. I asked her if our furniture had suffered any damage; she replied that there had been such confusion, caused by the multitude of strangers who had come to help, that she could not be sure of anything. I trembled for our money, which was contained in a small chest. I quickly went to Chaillot. Useless haste; the chest had already disappeared. I then experienced that one can love money without being miserly. This loss penetrated me with such acute pain that I thought I would lose my reason. I suddenly understood to what new misfortunes I was going to find myself exposed; poverty was the least of them. I knew Manon; I had already too much experienced that, however faithful and attached she might be to me in good fortune, one could not count on her in misery. She loved abundance and pleasures too much to sacrifice them for me: I would lose her, I cried out.

57.translation: Unhappy Knight, you are going to lose everything you love again! This thought threw me into such a dreadful state that I hesitated for a moment, wondering if I wouldn't be better off ending all my miseries with death. However, I retained enough presence of mind to want to examine first if there remained no resource for me. Heaven gave me birth to an idea that stopped my despair. I believed it wouldn't be impossible for me to hide our loss from Manon, and that, through industry or some favor of chance, I could provide quite honestly for her maintenance to prevent her from feeling necessity. I told myself to console myself that twenty thousand écus would be enough for us for ten years. Suppose the ten years are over, and none of the changes I hoped for have happened in my family. What course would I take? I don't know too well, but what I would do then, what prevents me from doing it today? How many people live in Paris who have neither my mind nor my natural qualities, and who must nevertheless their maintenance to their talents, such as they have! Providence, I added, reflecting on the different states of life, hasn't arranged things very wisely? Most of the great and the rich are fools: that is clear to those who know the world a little.

58.translation: Now there is admirable justice in this: if they combined intelligence with wealth, they would be too happy, and the rest of men too miserable. The qualities of the body and soul are accorded to these people, as means to extricate themselves from misery and poverty. Some share in the wealth of the great by serving their pleasures: they make dupes of them; others serve their instruction: they try to make honest men of them; it is true, it is rare that they succeed in this, but that is not the goal of divine Wisdom: they always derive a benefit from their needs, which is to live at the expense of those they instruct, and however one looks at it, it is an excellent source of income for the little people, the stupidity of the rich and great. These thoughts restored my heart and head a little. I first resolved to consult M. Lescaut, brother of Manon. He knew Paris perfectly, and I had had too many opportunities to recognize that it was neither from his wealth nor from the king's pay that he drew his clearest income. I had barely twenty pistoles left that had fortunately been found in my pocket. I showed him my purse, explaining my misfortune and my fears, and I asked him if there was a choice for me between dying of hunger or breaking my head with despair.

vocabulary.translation: of, from, or like God or a god

59.translation: He replied that racking one's brains was the recourse of fools; that to die of hunger, there were plenty of clever people who found themselves reduced to that, when they didn't want to make use of their talents; that it was up to me to examine what I was capable of; that he assured me of his help and advice in all my undertakings. That is very vague, Monsieur Lescaut, I said to him; my needs would require a more immediate remedy, for what do you want me to say to Manon? Speaking of Manon, he resumed, what is troubling you? Haven't you always, with her, the means to end your worries whenever you wish? A girl like her should support you, you and I.
