He cut off the reply that such impertinence deserved, to continue telling me that he guaranteed me a thousand écus to share between us before evening, if I wanted to follow his advice; that he knew a lord so liberal when it came to pleasures, that he was sure that a thousand écus would cost him nothing to obtain the favors of a girl like Manon. I stopped him. I had a better opinion of you, I replied to him; I had imagined that the reason you had for granting me your friendship was a feeling completely opposite to the one you are in now. He confessed impudently that he had always thought the same, and that, his sister having once violated the laws of her sex, although in favor of the man he loved most, he had only reconciled with her in the hope of profiting from her bad conduct.

60.translation: It was easy for me to judge that until then we had been his dupes. However much emotion this discourse might have caused me, the need I had for him obliged me to answer, laughing, that his advice was a last resort that had to be put off until the extreme. I asked him to open up some other path for me. He proposed that I take advantage of my youth and the advantageous features that I had received from nature, to connect with some old and generous lady. I did not like this option either, which would have made me unfaithful to Manon. I spoke to him about gambling, as the easiest means, and the most suitable to my situation. He told me that gambling, indeed, was a resource, but that it needed to be explained; that undertaking to play simply, with common hopes, was the true way to complete my ruin; that pretending to exercise alone, and without being supported, the little means that a clever man uses to correct fortune, was too dangerous a profession; that there was a third way, which was that of association, but that my youth made him fear that the gentlemen Confederates would not judge me to have the qualities proper for the League. He nevertheless promised his good offices with them; and what I would not have expected from him, he offered me some money, when I should find myself pressed by need.

vocabulary.translation: people who are deceived or tricked
resource or resort (in this context, a last resort)
Confederates (referring to a group of gamblers)

61.translation: The only favor I asked of him, under the circumstances, was not to tell Manon about the loss I had suffered, or about the subject of our conversation. I left his house even less satisfied than when I had entered; I even regretted having confided my secret to him. He had done nothing for me that I couldn't have obtained just as well without this disclosure, and I was terribly afraid that he would break his promise not to reveal anything to Manon. I also had reason to fear, from his declaration of feelings, that he might form the plan to take advantage of her, in his own words, by snatching her from my hands, or at least by advising her to leave me for some richer and happier lover. I made a thousand reflections on this, which only served to torment me and renew the despair I had felt in the morning. Several times I thought of writing to my father, and of pretending a new conversion, to obtain some financial help from him; but I immediately remembered that, despite all his kindness, he had confined me for six months in a narrow prison for my first fault; I was quite sure that after a scandal such as my flight from Saint-Sulpice must have caused, he would treat me much more severely.

62.translation: Finally, this confusion of thoughts produced one that suddenly brought calm back to my mind, and which I was surprised not to have had earlier, which was to turn to my friend Tiberge, in whom I was quite certain of always finding the same foundation of zeal and friendship. Nothing is more admirable, and does more honor to virtue, than the confidence with which one approaches people whose integrity one knows perfectly. One feels that there is no risk to take. If they are not always in a position to offer help, one is sure to at least get kindness and compassion from them. The heart, which closes with such care to the rest of men, opens naturally in their presence, like a flower blossoms in the light of the sun, which it expects only a gentle influence from. I regarded it as an effect of Heaven's protection to have remembered Tiberge so opportunely, and I resolved to find ways to see him before the end of the day. I returned immediately to my lodgings to write him a note, and to mark a place suitable for our conversation. I recommended silence and discretion to him, as one of the most important services he could render me in the situation of my affairs.

vocabulary.translation: to turn to, to resort to

63.translation: The joy that the hope of seeing him inspired in me erased the traces of sorrow that Manon would undoubtedly have noticed on my face. I spoke to her of our misfortune at Chaillot as if it were a trivial matter that should not alarm her; and since Paris was the place in the world where she took the most pleasure, she was not displeased to hear me say that it would be appropriate to remain there until some minor effects of the fire at Chaillot had been repaired. An hour later, I received Tiberge's response, in which he promised to come to the designated meeting place. I went there impatiently. Nevertheless, I felt some shame at appearing before the eyes of a friend whose presence alone should have been a reproach for my disorders, but my opinion of the goodness of his heart and my concern for Manon sustained my boldness. I had asked him to meet me at the garden of the Palais-Royal. He was there before me. He came to embrace me as soon as he saw me. He held me tightly in his arms for a long time, and I felt my face wet with his tears. I told him that I came to him only with embarrassment, and that I carried in my heart a keen feeling of my ingratitude; that the first thing I implored of him was to tell me whether I was still permitted to regard him as my friend, after having so justly deserved to lose his esteem and his affection.

vocabulary.translation: a trivial matter or something of little importance

64.translation: He replied to me in the tenderest tone that nothing could make him renounce this quality; that my misfortunes themselves, and if I allowed him to say so, my faults and disorders, had doubled his tenderness for me; but that it was a tenderness mixed with the most lively pain, such as one feels for a dear person, whom one sees approaching their ruin without being able to help them. We sat down on a bench. Alas! I said to him, with a sigh that came from the depths of my heart, your compassion must be excessive, my dear Tiberge; if you assure me that it equals my pains. I am ashamed to let you see them, for I confess that the cause is not glorious, but the effect is so sad that it is not necessary to love me as much as you do to be moved by them. He asked me, as a mark of friendship, to tell him without disguise what had happened to me since my departure from Saint-Sulpice. I satisfied him; and far from altering the truth in any way, or diminishing my faults to make them seem more excusable, I spoke to him of my passion with all the force it inspired in me. I represented it to him as one of those particular blows of fate that attach to the ruin of a wretch, and which virtue is as powerless to defend against as wisdom has been to foresee them.

65.translation: I gave him a vivid picture of my agitation, my fears, the despair I was in two hours before seeing him, and the one I would fall back into if I were abandoned by my friends as pitilessly as by fortune; finally, I moved the good Tiberge so much that I saw him as afflicted by compassion as I was by the feeling of my sorrows. He never tired of embracing me and exhorting me to take courage and consolation, but, as he always supposed that I had to separate from Manon, I made it clear to him that it was this separation itself that I regarded as the greatest of my misfortunes, and that I was willing to suffer, not only the extreme of misery, but the cruelest death, rather than receive a remedy more unbearable than all my ills together. Explain yourself, then, he said to me: what kind of help am I capable of giving you if you rebel against all my proposals? I did not dare to tell him that it was his purse that I needed. He understood it in the end, and having confessed that he believed he understood me, he remained suspended for some time, with the air of a person who hesitates. Do not believe, he resumed soon after, that my reverie comes from a cooling of zeal and friendship.

vocabulary.translation: a state of anxiety or nervous excitement
to urge strongly, to advise earnestly
a state of being lost in thought, daydreaming

66.translation: But to what alternative are you reducing me, if I must refuse the only help you wish to accept or if I must violate my duty by granting it? For is it not taking part in your disorder to make you persist in it? However, he continued after reflecting for a moment, I imagine that it is perhaps the violent state in which poverty throws you that does not leave you enough freedom to choose the best course; one needs a tranquil mind to appreciate wisdom and truth. I will find a way to get you some money. Allow me, my dear Knight, he added, embracing me, to only put one condition to this: that you tell me the place of your residence, and that you allow me at least to make my efforts to bring you back to virtue, which I know you love and from which only the violence of your passions is keeping you away. I sincerely granted him everything he wished, and I asked him to pity the malignity of my fate, which made me so poorly benefit from the advice of such a virtuous friend. He immediately took me to a banker he knew, who advanced me a hundred pistoles on his note, for it was by no means in cash. I have already said that he was not rich. His benefice was worth a thousand écus, but since it was the first year he possessed it, he had not yet touched any of the income: it was on future fruits that he was making this advance to me.

vocabulary.translation: a choice between two or more possibilities
benefit, income (in this context, a church position with income)

67.translation: I felt the full value of her generosity. I was touched by it, to the point of lamenting the blindness of a fatal love that made me violate all my duties. Virtue had enough strength for a few moments to rise in my heart against my passion, and I at least perceived, in that moment of clarity, the shame and indignity of my chains. But this struggle was slight and lasted only a short while. The sight of Manon would have made me fall from heaven, and I marveled, finding myself near her, that I could have for a moment considered as shameful a tenderness so just for an object so charming. Manon was a creature of extraordinary character. Never was a girl less attached to money than she, but she could not be at peace for a moment, with the fear of lacking it. It was pleasure and pastimes that she needed. She would never have wanted to touch a penny, if one could amuse oneself without it costing anything. She didn't even inquire what the source of our wealth was, provided she could pleasantly pass the day, so that, being neither excessively given to gambling nor capable of being dazzled by the splendor of great expenses, nothing was easier than to satisfy her, by creating for her each day amusements to her taste. But it was so necessary for her to be thus occupied by pleasure that there was no reason at all, otherwise, to base anything on her temperament and inclinations.

68.translation: Although she loved me tenderly, and I was the only one, as she readily admitted, who could make her fully taste the delights of love, I was almost certain that her tenderness would not hold against certain fears. She would have preferred me to all the world with a modest fortune; but I had no doubt that she would abandon me for some new B... when I would have nothing left to offer but constancy and fidelity. I therefore resolved to manage my personal expenses so well that I would always be able to provide for hers, and to deprive myself of a thousand necessities rather than limit even for superfluities. The carriage frightened me more than anything else; for there was no appearance of being able to keep horses and a coachman. I revealed my distress to M. Lescaut. I had not hidden from him that I had received a hundred pistoles from a friend. He repeated to me that, if I wanted to try my luck at gambling, he did not despair that by gracefully sacrificing a hundred francs to treat his associates, I might be admitted, by his recommendation, into the League of Industry. Whatever repugnance I had to deceive, I allowed myself to be carried away by a cruel necessity. M. Lescaut presented me that same evening as one of his relatives; he added that I was all the more disposed to succeed because I needed the greatest favors of fortune.

69.translation: However, to let them know that my misery was not that of a nobody, he told them that I intended to give them supper. The offer was accepted. I treated them magnificently. We talked for a long time about the kindness of my appearance and my fortunate disposition. It was claimed that there was much to hope for from me, because having something in my physiognomy that smelled of an honest man, no one would suspect my tricks. Finally, thanks were given to Mr. Lescaut for providing the Order with a novice of my merit, and one of the knights was charged with giving me, for a few days, the necessary instructions. The main theater of my exploits was to be the Hotel of Transylvania, where there was a pharaoh table in one room and various other card and dice games in the gallery. This academy was held for the benefit of Mr. the Prince of R..., who was then residing at Clagny, and most of his officers were of our society. Shall I say it to my shame? I profited in a short time from the lessons of my master. I acquired especially great skill in making a volte-face, in dealing the card, and helping myself very well with a long pair of cuffs, I palmed quite easily to deceive the eyes of the most skillful, and ruin without affectation a quantity of honest players.

70.translation: This extraordinary address hastened so much the progress of my fortune, that I found myself in a few weeks with considerable sums, besides those that I shared in good faith with my associates. I no longer feared, then, to tell Manon of our loss at Chaillot, and, to console her in telling her this unfortunate news, I rented a furnished house, where we established ourselves with an air of opulence and security. Tiberge had not failed, during that time, to render me frequent visits. His morality never ended. He constantly began again to represent to me the wrong that I did to my conscience, to my honor and to my fortune. I received his advice with friendship, and although I had not the least disposition to follow them, I knew how to be grateful for his zeal, because I knew its source. Sometimes I pleasantly mocked him, in the very presence of Manon, and I exhorted him not to be more scrupulous than a great number of bishops and other priests, who know how to reconcile a mistress with a benefice. See, I said to him, showing him the eyes of mine, and tell me if there are faults that are not justified by such a beautiful cause. He took patience. He even pushed it quite far; but when he saw that my riches were increasing, and that not only had I returned to him his hundred pistoles, but having rented a new house and doubled my expense, I was going to plunge myself more than ever into pleasures, he completely changed his tone and manner.

71.translation: He complained of my hardness of heart; he threatened me with the punishments of Heaven, and he predicted some of the misfortunes that were not long in coming to me. It is impossible, he said to me, that the wealth that serves to maintain your disorders came to you by legitimate means. You acquired them unjustly; they will be taken from you in the same way. The most terrible punishment of God would be to let you enjoy them peacefully. All my advice, he added, have been useless to you; I foresee only too well that they would soon become burdensome to you. Farewell, ungrateful and weak friend. May your criminal pleasures vanish like a shadow! May your fortune and money perish without recourse, and leave you alone and naked, to feel the vanity of the goods that have foolishly intoxicated you! It is then that you will find me disposed to love and serve you, but I break today all commerce with you, and I detest the life you lead. It was in my room, in the presence of Manon, that he delivered this apostolic harangue to me. He rose to withdraw. I wanted to detain him, but I was stopped by Manon, who told me that he was a madman that had to be allowed to go out. His discourse did fail to make some impression on me. I thus note the various occasions where my heart felt a return to goodness, because it is to this memory that I owed later a part of my strength in the most unfortunate circumstances of my life.

72.translation: Manon's caresses dispelled, in a moment, the sorrow that this scene had caused me. We continued to lead a life entirely composed of pleasure and love. The increase of our wealth doubled our affection; Venus and Fortune had no happier and more tender slaves. Gods! why call the world a place of miseries, since one can taste such charming delights there? But, alas! their weakness is that they pass too quickly. What other happiness would one want to propose if they were meant to last forever? Ours had the common fate, that is to say, of lasting little, and being followed by bitter regrets. I had made, at gambling, such considerable gains, that I was thinking of investing a part of my money. My servants did not ignore my successes, especially my valet and Manon's maid, before whom we often spoke without distrust. This girl was pretty; my valet was in love with her. They had to deal with young and easy-going masters, whom they imagined they could deceive easily. They conceived the plan, and they executed it so unfortunately for us, that they put us in a state from which we have never been able to recover. M. Lescaut having given us to supper one day, it was about midnight when we returned home. I called my valet, and Manon her maid; neither one nor the other appeared.

73.translation: We were told that they had not been seen in the house since eight o'clock, and that they had left after having some crates transported, according to the orders they said they had received from me. I sensed part of the truth, but I formed no suspicions that were not surpassed by what I saw upon entering my room. The lock of my study had been forced, and my money taken, along with all my clothes. While I was reflecting alone on this incident, Manon came, quite frightened, to tell me that the same devastation had been wrought in her apartment. The blow seemed so cruel that only an extraordinary effort of reason prevented me from giving way to cries and tears. The fear of communicating my despair to Manon made me affect to take on a calm face. I told her, jokingly, that I would take revenge on some dupe at the Hotel of Transylvania. However, she seemed so sensitive to our misfortune that her sadness had much more power to afflict me than my feigned joy had had to prevent her from being too dejected. We are lost! she said to me, with tears in her eyes. I tried in vain to console her with my caresses; my own tears betrayed my despair and consternation. Indeed, we were absolutely ruined, to the point that we didn't even have a shirt left.

74.translation: I decided to send for M. Lescaut immediately. He advised me to go right away to see the Lieutenant of Police and the Chief Provost of Paris. I went, but it was to my great misfortune; because not only did this step and the ones I had these two justice officers take produce nothing, but I gave Lescaut time to talk to his sister, and to inspire in her, during my absence, a horrible resolution. He spoke to her of M. de G... M..., an old voluptuary who paid lavishly for pleasures, and he made her consider so many advantages in placing herself under his protection, that, disturbed as she was by our disgrace, she agreed to everything he undertook to persuade her. This honorable bargain was concluded before my return, and its execution postponed until the next day, after Lescaut had warned M. de G... M... I found him waiting for me at our lodgings; but Manon had gone to bed in her apartment, and she had ordered her lackey to tell me that, needing a little rest, she asked me to let her be alone that night. Lescaut left after offering me a few pistolas which I accepted. It was nearly four o'clock when I went to bed, and having still occupied for a long time with the means of restoring my fortune, I fell asleep so late that I could not wake up until eleven o'clock or noon.

75.translation: I got up quickly to go inquire about Manon's health; I was told that she had left an hour before with her brother, who had come to get her in a hired carriage. Although such an outing with Lescaut seemed mysterious to me, I forced myself to suspend my suspicions. I let a few hours pass, which I spent reading. Finally, no longer being master of my anxiety, I paced back and forth in our apartments. I noticed, in Manon's room, a sealed letter that was on her table. The address was to me, and the writing was in her hand. I opened it with a deadly shudder; it read as follows: I swear to you, my dear Chevalier, that you are the idol of my heart and that there is no one in the world but whom I can love the way I love you; but don't you see, my poor dear soul, that in the state to which we are reduced, fidelity is a foolish virtue? Do you believe that one can be very tender when one lacks bread? Hunger would cause me some fatal mistake; I would one day breathe my last breath, believing I was breathing one of love. I adore you, count on that; but let me, for some time, the management of our fortune. Woe to whoever falls into my nets! I am working to make my Chevalier rich and happy.

76.translation: My brother will bring you news of your Manon, and that she cried at the necessity of leaving you. I remained, after this reading, in a state that would be difficult for me to describe because I still do not know today what kind of feelings I was then agitated by. It was one of those unique situations to which one has experienced nothing similar. One cannot explain them to others, because they do not have the idea of them; and one has difficulty in sorting them out for oneself, because being alone of their kind, they do not connect to anything in memory, and cannot even be compared to any known feeling. However, whatever nature mine were, it is certain that there must have entered into them the pain of spite, jealousy, and shame. Happy if there had not entered into them even more love! She loves me, I want to believe; but would it not be necessary, I cried out, that she be a monster to hate me? What rights has one ever had over a heart that I do not have over hers? What remains for me to do for her, after all that I have sacrificed for her? However, she abandons me! and the ungrateful one believes herself safe from my reproaches by telling me that she does not cease to love me! She fears hunger. Love's God! What coarseness of feelings!

77.translation: and that is not responding well to my delicacy! I did not apprehend her, I who so willingly expose myself for her by renouncing my fortune and the comforts of my father's house; I who have deprived myself even of necessities to satisfy her little moods and whims. She adores me, she says. If you adored me, ungrateful woman, I know well whose advice you would have taken; you would not have left me, at least, without saying goodbye. It is I who must be asked what cruel pains one feels in separating from what one adores. One must have lost one's mind to expose oneself to that voluntarily. My complaints were interrupted by a visit I did not expect. It was that of Lescaut. Executioner! I said to him, drawing my sword, where is Manon? what have you done with her? This movement frightened him; he replied that if this was how I received him when he came to give me an account of the most considerable service he could have rendered me, he would withdraw and never set foot in my house again. I rushed to the bedroom door, which I carefully closed. Do not imagine, I said to him, turning to him, that you can take me for a fool again and deceive me with fables. You must defend your life, or restore Manon to me.

78.translation: There! You are so quick-witted!" he replied; "that's the only subject that brings me here. I've come to announce a happiness you wouldn't expect, and for which you might perhaps recognize that you owe me something. I wanted to be enlightened on the spot. He told me that Manon, unable to bear the fear of poverty, and especially the idea of being suddenly obliged to reform our establishment, had asked him to introduce her to M. de G... M..., who was reputed to be a generous man. He was careful not to tell me that the advice had come from him, nor that he had prepared the way before leading her there. "I took her there this morning," he continued, "and that honorable man was so charmed by her merits that he invited her first to keep him company at his country house, where he has gone to spend a few days." "As for me," added Lescaut, "who suddenly understood what advantage this could be for you, I skillfully made her understand that Manon had suffered considerable losses, and I so pricked his generosity that he began by giving her a present of two hundred pistoles. I told him that was honorable for the present, but that the future would bring great needs to my sister; that she had moreover taken charge of a young brother, who had been left in our care after the death of our father and mother, and that if he thought her worthy of his esteem, he would not let her suffer in this poor child whom she regarded as half of herself.

vocabulary.translation: to bear, to support, to endure

79.translation: This account did not fail to move him. He has undertaken to rent a convenient house for you and for Manon, for it is you yourself who are this poor little orphan brother. He has promised to furnish it properly for you, and to provide you with four hundred good pounds each month, which will make, if I calculate correctly, four thousand eight hundred at the end of each year. He has given orders to his steward, before leaving for his country estate, to find a house, and to have it ready for his return. You will then see Manon again, who has charged me to kiss you a thousand times for her, and to assure you that she loves you more than ever. I sat down, dreaming of this strange arrangement of my fate. I found myself in a division of feelings, and consequently in an uncertainty so difficult to resolve that I remained a long time without answering many questions that Lescaut was asking me one after another. It was at this moment that honor and virtue made me feel once again the pricks of remorse, and that I cast my eyes, sighing, toward Amiens, toward my father's house, toward Saint-Sulpice and toward all the places where I had lived in innocence. By what immense space was I not separated from that happy state! I no longer saw it but from afar, like a shadow that still attracted my regrets and my desires, but too weak to excite my efforts.

80.translation: By what fatality, I said, have I become so criminal? Love is an innocent passion; how has it changed for me into a source of miseries and disorders? Who prevented me from living tranquil and virtuous with Manon? Why didn't I marry her before obtaining anything of her love? My father, who loved me so tenderly, wouldn't he have consented if I had pressed him with legitimate instances? Ah! my father would have loved her himself, as a charming girl, too worthy to be the wife of his son; I would be happy with Manon's love, with my father's affection, with the esteem of honest people, with the goods of fortune and the tranquility of virtue. Fatal reversal! What is the infamous character that comes here to propose to me? What! I will go share... But is there to hesitate if it is Manon who has arranged it, and if I lose her without this compliance? Monsieur Lescaut, I cried out, closing my eyes, as if to push away such sorrowful reflections, if you have intended to serve me I thank you. You could have taken a more honest way; but it's a finished thing, isn't it? Let's therefore think only of taking advantage of your care and fulfilling your project. Lescaut, to whom my anger, followed by a very long silence, had caused embarrassment, was delighted to see me take a party completely different from the one he had apprehended no doubt; he was nothing less than brave, and I had better proofs of it later.

81.translation: "Yes, yes," he hastened to reply to me, "it's a very good service I have rendered you, and you will see that we will derive more advantage from it than you expect. We discussed how we could prevent the suspicions that M. de G... M... might conceive of our friendship, seeing me taller and perhaps a little older than he imagined. We found no other means than to appear simple and provincial before him, and to make him believe that I intended to enter the ecclesiastical state, and that I was going every day to the college for that purpose. We also resolved that I would pretend to be very ill the first time I was admitted to the honor of greeting him. He returned to town three or four days later; he himself conducted Manon to the house that his steward had taken care to prepare. She immediately had Lescaut informed of her return; and he having given me notice, we both went to her place. The old lover had already left. Despite the resignation with which I had submitted to his will, I could not suppress the murmur of my heart at seeing her again. I appeared sad and languishing to her. The joy of finding her again did not quite overcome the sorrow of her infidelity."

82.translation: She, on the contrary, seemed transported with pleasure at seeing me again. She reproached me for my coldness. I couldn't help but call her perfidious and unfaithful, which I accompanied with as many sighs. At first, she mocked my simplicity; but when she saw my gaze always sadly fixed on her, and the pain I had in digesting such a change contrary to my temperament and desires, she went alone into her study. I followed her a moment later. I found her there in tears; I asked her what caused them. It's very easy for you to see, she said to me, how do you expect me to live, if my sight is only capable of causing you a dark and sorrowful expression? You haven't given me a single caress since you've been here for an hour, and you received mine with the majesty of the Great Turk in the Seraglio. Listen, Manon, I replied to her, embracing her, I cannot hide from you that my heart is mortally afflicted. I am not speaking now of the alarms your unexpected flight threw me into, nor of the cruelty you had in abandoning me without a word of consolation, after having spent the night in a bed other than mine. The charm of your presence would make me forget it all the more.

83.translation: But do you think I can think without sighs, and even without tears, I continued as I shed a few for the sad and unhappy life you want me to lead in this house? Let's put my birth and my honor aside: these are no longer such weak reasons that should compete with a love like mine; but this love itself, don't you imagine that it groans to see itself so poorly rewarded, or rather so cruelly treated by an ungrateful and hard mistress?... She interrupted me: here, she said, my Knight, it's useless to torment me with reproaches that pierce my heart when they come from you. I see what hurts you. I had hoped that you would consent to the project I had made to restore a bit of our fortune, and it was to spare your delicacy that I had begun to execute it without your participation; but I renounce it, since you do not approve of it. She added that she only asked me for a little complaisance for the rest of the day; that she had already received two hundred pistoles from her old lover, and that he had promised to bring her in the evening a beautiful pearl necklace with other jewels, and on top of that, half of the annual pension he had promised her. Just leave me the time, she told me, to receive his presents; I swear to you that he cannot boast of the advantages I have given him over me, for I have kept him away from the city until now.

84.translation: It is true that he has kissed my hands more than a million times; it is just that he pay for this pleasure, and five or six thousand francs will not be too much, proportioning the price to his wealth and his age. His resolution pleased me much more than the hope of the five thousand pounds. I had occasion to recognize that my heart had not yet lost all sense of honor since it was so satisfied to escape infamy. But I was born for short joys and long pains. Fortune will deliver me from one precipice only to make me fall into another. When I had shown Manon, by a thousand caresses, how happy I believed myself to be at her change, I told her that it was necessary to inform M. Lescaut, so that our measures might be taken in concert. He murmured at first; but the four or five thousand pounds of ready money made him cheerfully enter into our views. It was therefore arranged that we would all meet to supper with M. de G... M..., and this for two reasons: one, to give us the pleasure of a pleasant scene by making me pass for a schoolboy, brother of Manon; the other, to prevent that old libertine from emancipating himself too much with my mistress, by the right he would believe he had acquired in paying so liberally in advance.

vocabulary.translation: libertine, a person who is morally unrestrained

85.translation: We were to withdraw, Lescaut and I, when he would go up to the room where he intended to spend the night; and Manon, instead of following him, promised to come out and spend the time with me. Lescaut took care to have exactly a carriage at the door. The supper hour having come, Mr. de G... M... did not keep us waiting long. Lescaut was with his sister in the hall. The old man's first compliment was to offer his beautiful niece a necklace of bracelets and pearl pendants, which were worth at least a thousand écus. He then counted out to her, in fine gold louis, the sum of two thousand four hundred livres, which made up half of her pension. He seasoned his gift with a quantity of sweetmeats in the style of the old court. Manon could not refuse him a few kisses; they were so many rights she acquired over the money he put into her hands. I was at the door, where I was listening, waiting for Lescaut to warn me to enter. He came and took me by the hand when Manon had secured the money and jewels, and leading me toward Mr. de G... M..., he ordered me to bow to him. I made two or three of the deepest bows. 'Excuse him, sir,' said Lescaut, 'he is a very new child. He is far, as you see, from having the airs of Paris; but we hope that a little practice will shape him.'

86.translation: You will often have the honor of seeing him here, he added, turning to me; make good use of such a fine example. The old man seemed pleased to see me. He gave me two or three little pats on the cheek, telling me I was a pretty boy, but that I must be on my guard in Paris, where young people easily give themselves over to debauchery. Lescaut assured him that I was naturally so sensible that I spoke only of becoming a priest, and that all my pleasure was in making small chapels. I find he has Manon's air, the old man replied, lifting my chin with his hand. I answered with a silly air: Sir, it is because our two natures are very closely related; moreover, I love my sister Manon as another myself. Do you hear him? he said to Lescaut, he has wit. It's a pity that child doesn't have a little more sense. Ho! sir, I replied, I have seen many people in our churches, and I believe I shall find, in Paris, some more foolish than myself. See, he added, this is admirable for a provincial child. Our entire conversation was pretty much of this kind. During supper, Manon, who was playful, came close to ruining everything several times with her bursts of laughter.

vocabulary.translation: to profit, to make use of

87.translation: I found the opportunity, during supper, to tell him his own story, and bad luck threatened him. Lescaut and Manon trembled during my narration, especially when I portrayed him naturally; but pride prevented him from recognizing himself, and I finished it so skillfully that he was the first to find it very laughable. You will see that it is not without reason that I have dwelt on this ridiculous scene. Finally, when the time for sleep arrived, he spoke of love and impatience. Lescaut and I withdrew; he was conducted to his chamber, and Manon, having gone out under the pretext of a need, came to join us at the door. The carriage, which was waiting for us three or four houses further down, advanced to receive us. We instantly left the neighborhood. Although in my own eyes this action was a genuine piece of roguery, it was not the most unjust that I believed I had to reproach myself with. I had more scruples about the money I had acquired through gambling. However, we profited as little from one as from the other, and Heaven permitted that the lesser of these two injustices be the more rigorously punished. M. de G... M... did not long delay in perceiving that he had been duped. I do not know if he made any efforts that very evening to discover us, but he had enough influence not to make long useless ones, and we enough imprudence to count too much on the greatness of Paris and on the distance there was between our neighborhood and his.

88.translation: Not only was he informed of our dwelling and our present affairs, but he also learned who I was, the life I had lived in Paris, the former connection of Manon with B..., the deception she had practiced on him, in a word, all the scandalous parts of our story. He thereupon resolved to have us arrested and to treat us less like criminals than like confirmed libertines. We were still in bed, when a police officer entered our chamber with a half-dozen guards. They first seized our money, or rather that of M. de G... M..., and having made us get up abruptly, they conducted us to the door, where we found two carriages, in one of which the poor Manon was carried off without explanation, and I dragged into the other to Saint-Lazare. One must have experienced such reverses, to judge of the despair they can cause. Our guards had the hardness not to allow me to embrace Manon, nor to say a word to her. I long did not know what had become of her. It was doubtless a happiness for me not to have known it at first, for such a terrible catastrophe would have made me lose my senses and, perhaps, my life. My unfortunate mistress was therefore carried off, before my eyes, and conducted to a retreat that I have horror of naming.

89.translation: What fate for such a charming creature, who could have occupied the first throne in the world, if all men had had my eyes and my heart! She was not treated barbarously there; but she was confined in a narrow prison, alone, and condemned to fulfill a certain task of work each day, as a necessary condition to obtain some disgusting food. I only learned this sad detail long after, when I myself had endured several months of a harsh and tedious penance. My guards had not informed me either of the place where they had orders to take me, so I only learned my destiny at the door of Saint-Lazare. I would have preferred death at that moment to the state I believed I was about to fall into. I had terrible ideas about that house. My fear increased when upon entering the guards searched my pockets a second time, to make sure I had no weapons, no means of defense. The superior appeared immediately; he had been forewarned of my arrival; he greeted me with great gentleness. 'Father,' I said to him, 'no indignities. I would lose a thousand lives before suffering one.' 'No, no,' he replied to me, 'you will take wise conduct, and we will be content with one another.' He asked me to go up to a high chamber. I followed him without resistance.

90.translation: The archers accompanied us to the gate, and the superior entered with me, signaling to them to withdraw. 'So I am your prisoner!' I said to him. 'Well, Father, what do you intend to do with me?' He told me he was pleased to see me adopt a reasonable tone; that his duty would be to inspire in me a taste for virtue and religion, and mine to benefit from his exhortations and advice; that if I were but willing to respond to the attentions he would show me, I would find nothing but pleasure in my solitude. 'Ah! pleasure!' I retorted; 'you don't know, Father, the one thing that is capable of making me taste it!' 'I know,' he replied; 'but I hope your inclination will change.' His reply made me understand that he was informed of my adventures, and perhaps of my name. I asked him to enlighten me. He naturally told me that he had been informed of everything. This knowledge was the cruelest of all my punishments. I began to pour a stream of tears, with all the signs of dreadful despair. I could not console myself for a humiliation that would make me the laughingstock of all my acquaintances, and the shame of my family. Thus I spent eight days in the deepest dejection, unable to hear anything or occupy myself with anything other than my disgrace.

vocabulary.translation: superior (the head of a religious institution)
a person's natural tendency or urge to act or feel in a particular way

91.translation: The very memory of Manon added nothing to my pain. It entered into it, at least, only as a sentiment that had preceded this new sorrow, and the dominant passion of my soul was shame and confusion. There are few people who know the strength of these particular movements of the heart. Most men are only sensitive to five or six passions, within the circle of which their life passes, and in which all their agitations are reduced. Take away from them love and hatred, pleasure and pain, hope and fear, and they feel nothing more. But people of a more noble character can be moved in a thousand different ways; it seems that they have more than five senses, and that they can receive ideas and sensations that pass the ordinary bounds of nature; and as they have a feeling of this greatness that elevates them above the common, there is nothing of which they are more jealous. Hence it is that they suffer so impatiently contempt and ridicule, and that shame is one of their most violent passions. I had this sad advantage at Saint-Lazare. My sadness appeared so excessive to the superior that, apprehending the consequences, he believed he ought to treat me with great gentleness and indulgence. He visited me two or three times a day.

92.translation: He often took me with him for a turn in the garden, and his zeal exhausted itself in exhortations and salutary advice. I received them with gentleness; I even showed him my gratitude. He drew from this the hope of my conversion. 'You have such a gentle and amiable nature,' he said to me one day, 'that I cannot understand the disorders of which you are accused. Two things astonish me: one, how, with such good qualities, you could have given yourself up to the excess of libertinism; and the other which I admire even more, how you so willingly receive my counsel and instructions, after having lived several years in the habit of disorder. If it is repentance, you are a striking example of the mercies of Heaven; if it is natural goodness, you have at least an excellent foundation of character, which makes me hope that we will not need to keep you here long, to bring you back to an honest and regulated life.' I was delighted to see this opinion of me. I resolved to increase it by a conduct that could satisfy him completely, convinced that it was the surest means of shortening my prison. I asked him for books. He was surprised that, having left me the choice of those I wanted to read, I decided on some serious authors. I pretended to apply myself to study with the greatest attachment, and thus I gave him, on all occasions, proofs of the change he desired.

93.translation: However, it was only outward. I must confess to my shame, I played the role of a hypocrite at Saint-Lazare. Instead of studying, when I was alone, I only occupied myself with lamenting my destiny; I cursed my prison and the tyranny that kept me there. I had no sooner had some relief from the distress that had overwhelmed me than I fell back into the torments of love. The absence of Manon, the uncertainty of her fate, the fear of never seeing her again were the sole object of my sad meditations. I pictured her in the arms of G... M..., for that was the thought I had had at first; and, far from imagining that he had treated her as he had treated me, I was convinced that he had only had me removed so that he could possess her peacefully. I thus passed days and nights whose length seemed eternal to me. I had no hope but in the success of my hypocrisy. I carefully observed the face and words of the superior to ascertain what he thought of me, and I made it my study to please him, as if he were the arbiter of my destiny. I easily recognized that I was completely in his good graces. I no longer doubted that he was disposed to help me. I one day took the boldness to ask him whether it was from him that my release depended.

vocabulary.translation: a person who pretends to have virtues or beliefs they do not actually possess

94.translation: He told me that he was not absolutely the master of the situation, but that, based on his testimony, he hoped that Mr. de G... M..., at whose request the Lieutenant General of Police had had me imprisoned, would consent to restore my freedom. 'May I flatter myself,' I asked gently, 'that two months of imprisonment, which I have already endured, will seem to him sufficient atonement?' He promised to speak to him about it if I wished. I earnestly asked him to do me this favor. Two days later, he informed me that G... M... had been so moved by the good things he had heard about me, that not only did he seem inclined to let me see the light of day, but he had even shown great desire to get to know me better, and that he proposed to visit me in my prison. Although his presence could not be pleasant to me, I regarded it as a sure step toward my freedom. He did indeed come to Saint-Lazare. I found his air more serious and less foolish than it had been in Manon's house. He spoke to me some sensible words about my misconduct. He added, apparently to justify his own disorders, that it was permissible for human weakness to seek certain pleasures that nature demands, but that knavery and shameful artifices deserved to be punished.

vocabulary.translation: reparation for a wrong or injury
having a tendency or disposition toward something

95.translation: I listened to him with an air of submission that seemed to satisfy him. I wasn't even offended when I heard him make some sarcastic remarks about my brotherhood with Lescaut and Manon, and about the little chapels he supposed, he told me, that I must have made a great number of at Saint-Lazare, since I found so much pleasure in this pious occupation. But unfortunately for him and for myself, he let slip that Manon would have surely made some very pretty ones at the Hospital too. Despite the shudder that the name of Hospital caused me, I still had the power to ask him gently to explain himself. 'Yes indeed!' he replied, 'it's two months now that she's learning wisdom at the General Hospital, and I wish she has derived as much profit from it as you did at Saint-Lazare. If I had had an eternal prison, or even death itself before my eyes, I would not have been master of my emotion at this dreadful news. I threw myself upon him with such dreadful rage that I lost half my strength. I had enough nevertheless to knock him to the ground, and to seize him by the throat. I was strangling him, when the noise of his fall, and some sharp cries that I barely allowed him the freedom to utter, attracted the superior and several religious men into my room.

96.translation: He was delivered from my hands. I had almost lost my own strength and breath. Oh God! I cried out, with a thousand sighs; justice of Heaven! Must I live a moment after such infamy? I wanted to throw myself again on the barbarian who had just assassinated me. They stopped me. My despair, my cries and my tears passed all imagination. I did such astonishing things that all the spectators, who ignored the cause, looked at each other with as much fear as surprise. Mr. de G... M... was adjusting his wig and cravat at that time, and despite having been so mistreated, he ordered the superior to bind me more tightly than ever, and to punish me with all the punishments known to be proper for Saint-Lazare. No, sir, the superior said to him; we do not treat a person of the birth of Mr. the Knight in this manner. He is so gentle, moreover, and so honest, that I find it hard to understand that he would have gone to such excess without strong reasons. This answer further disconcerted Mr. de G... M... He left saying that he knew how to bend both the superior and me, and all those who would dare to resist him. The superior, having ordered his religious to lead him away, remained alone with me.

97.translation: He begged me to quickly tell him where this disorder came from. 'Oh, my Father,' I said to him, continuing to cry like a child, 'imagine the most horrible cruelty, imagine the most detestable of all barbarities, it is the act that the unworthy G... M... had the cowardice to commit. Oh! He pierced my heart. I will never recover from it. I want to tell you everything,' I added, sobbing. 'You are good, you will have pity on me.' I gave him an abbreviated account of the long and insurmountable passion I had for Manon, of the flourishing state of our fortune before we had been stripped by our own servants, of the offers that G... M... had made to my mistress, of the conclusion of their bargain, and of the way it had been broken. I represented the things, in truth, from the most favorable side for us. 'That is,' I continued, 'from what source has come the zeal of Mr. G... M... for my conversion. He had the influence to have me confined here for a pure motive of revenge. I forgive him, but, my Father, that is not all: he cruelly had my dearest half carried away, he had her shamefully put in the Hospital, he had the audacity to announce it to me today from his own mouth.'

98.translation: At the Hospital, my Father! Oh Heaven! my charming mistress, my dear queen at the Hospital, like the most infamous of all creatures! Where will I find enough strength not to die of pain and shame? The good Father, seeing me in this excess of affliction, undertook to console me. He told me that he had never understood my adventure in the way I recounted it; that he had indeed known that I lived in disorder, but he had imagined that what had obliged Mr. de G... M... to take an interest in it, was some bond of esteem and friendship with my family; that he had only explained it to himself on that basis; that what I had just told him would bring great changes to my affairs, and that he did not doubt at all that the account he intended to give to Mr. the Lieutenant General of Police could contribute to my freedom. He then asked me why I had not yet thought to give news to my family, since they had had no part in my captivity. I satisfied this objection with some reasons taken from the pain I had apprehended causing my father, and the shame I would have felt myself. Finally he promised to go right away to the Lieutenant of Police, if only, he added, to prevent something worse from Mr.

99.translation: de G... M.... who left this house very dissatisfied, and who is sufficiently respected to be feared. I awaited the return of the Father with all the agitation of an unhappy man who is approaching the moment of his sentence. For me, it was an inexpressible torment to imagine Manon at the Hospital. Besides the infamy of this place, I did not know in what manner she was treated there, and the memory of some particulars that I had heard about this house of horror renewed my transports at every moment. I was so resolved to rescue her at any price and by any means possible, that I would have set fire to Saint-Lazare, if it had been impossible for me to leave otherwise. I therefore reflected on the paths I had to take, if it happened that the Lieutenant General of Police continued to keep me there against my will. I applied all my industry to all tests; I went through all possibilities. I saw nothing that could assure me of a certain escape, and I feared being confined more tightly if I made an unsuccessful attempt. I recalled the names of some friends, from whom I could hope for help; but what means to let them know my situation? Finally, I believed I had formed a plan so clever that it could succeed, and I resolved to arrange it better still after the return of the Superior Father, if the futility of his errand made it necessary.

100.translation: He did not delay in returning. I did not see on his face the marks of joy that accompany good news. 'I have spoken,' he told me, 'to the Lieutenant General of Police, but I spoke to him too late. M. de G... M... went to see him upon leaving here, and has so strongly prejudiced you against me, that he was about to send me new orders to tighten your confinement further. However, when I informed him of the true state of your affairs, he appeared to soften considerably, and laughing a little at the old M. de G... M...'s incontinence, he told me that it was necessary to leave you here for six months to satisfy him; all the better, he said, as this dwelling could not be useless to you. He recommended that I treat you honestly, and I assure you that you will have no cause to complain of my manners. This explanation from the good superior was long enough to give me time for a wise reflection. I conceived that I would risk thwarting my plans if I showed too much eagerness for my freedom. On the contrary, I indicated to him that in the necessity of remaining, it was a sweet consolation for me to have some place in his esteem. I then asked him, without affectation, to grant me a favor, which was of no importance to anyone, but which would greatly contribute to my tranquility; it was to have a friend of mine, a holy ecclesiastic who lived at Saint-Sulpice, informed that I was at Saint-Lazare, and to allow me to receive his visits occasionally.

vocabulary.translation: lack of self-restraint, especially regarding sexual desires
